entry.title: Re: How I can consume a project stock in Maintenance Orde...
entry.content:
Dear @Egbert_de_Jong,

Point 1. Is Project Stock (special stock Q) possible in Public Cloud for work orders / maintenance?

Yes but only if the WBS belongs to a project profile that supports project stock (valuated stock). SAP delivered (predefined) enterprise project profiles are set up like this Investment Project = Valuated Stock, Project with Revenue = Valuated Stock, while Overhead / Statistical = No Stock

SAP Notes below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3204437

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3557238

So:

  • If your WBS is in Investment Project or Project with Revenue you can hold and report Project Stock Q it will show in stock overviews as project stock

  • If your WBS is in Overhead Project or Statistical Project project stock is not enabled and postings/requests for project stock will be blocked

Point 2. Consuming project stock to a Maintenance Order is supported

For the withdrawal itself Movement type 261 GI for an order, including maintenance orders supports special stock indicator Q, so the technical capability exists the blocker is whether your WBS is allowed to have project stock from point 1

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3697643

Point 3. Why migration allowed project stock only for some projects/WBS

This is almost certainly because those project types map to different project profiles / inventory valuation:

  • The allowed ones correspond to Investment Project / Project with Revenue (valuated stock)

  • The blocked ones correspond to Overhead/Statistical (no stock)

SAP explicitly documents the restriction Account Assignment Category Q with a WBS outside Investment Project / Project with Revenue isn’t supported inventory valuation mismatch / no stock valuation

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3204437

Point 4. Workarounds

Option A: If you need inventory tracked as Project Stock stock + value by WBS

  • Use a project type/profile that supports it Investment Project or Project with Revenue

  • If you want to avoid AuC side effects, SAP even suggests a workaround to use an Investment Project with an investment profile like 000002 so no AuCs are generated

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3557238

Option B: If your projects must remain Overhead/Statistical but no project stock

Then you cannot keep inventory as Project Stock Q. Typical workaround is:

  • keep materials in plant unrestricted stock

  • issue them to the maintenance order/WBS so costs hit the WBS but inventory is not segregated as project stock

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Business Partner Master Data in SAP Cloud ERP 2602
entry.content:
In today's fast-paced digital economy, the integrity, security, and accessibility of master data are paramount to operational excellence. High-quality Business Partner data forms the foundation of countless critical processes, from procurement and sales to finance and compliance. I am pleased to share some of the latest innovations within SAP S/4HANA Cloud that are designed to elevate your business partner master data management strategy.

SAP Cloud ERP is part of SAP Business Suite that brings AI, data, and applications together into a fully integrated system. This community is open for anyone in the cloud ERP ecosystem–customers, partners, and SAP experts.  

To strengthen security and data integrity, we implement header-level authorization checks and proactive duplicate detection to prevent unauthorized access and ensure high-quality, reliable data from the outset. At the same time, onward replication across multisystem landscapes and conversational AI through Joule enable seamless synchronization and intuitive access to information, driving efficiency, transparency, and faster decision-making across the enterprise.


This post will provide an overview of four significant new features: 
1. Enhanced security through header-level authorization checks.
2. Proactive data quality management with an API-based duplicate check.
3. Streamlined data synchronization via onward replication in multisystem landscapes.
4. Efficient data access using our conversational AI, Joule.

1. Enable Business Partner Header-Level Authorization Checks Based on Data Controller and Purpose

Data privacy and compliance are non-negotiable. To provide more granular control over sensitive information, we have introduced the ability to enable authorization checks at the business partner header level based on the combination of a Data Controller and a Purpose.

Feature Scope:
This feature allows for the assignment of specific data controller and purpose combinations directly to a business partner record. Subsequently, the system can enforce authorization checks based on these assignments.

Key Benefits:
The primary advantage is a significant enhancement in access control. With this feature activated, a user must be explicitly authorized for the specific data controller and purpose assigned to a business partner to gain access to that record. This ensures that only relevant personnel can view or process sensitive data, strengthening your data governance and compliance posture in line with regulations like GDPR.

 

Business partner: assignment of purposes along with data controllers

2. Business Partner A2X OData API Operation for Duplicate Check

Maintaining a clean and duplicate-free master data repository is a constant challenge, especially in integrated environments. To address this proactively, we have extended the capabilities of our core Business Partner OData service.

Feature Scope:
A new operation for performing a duplicate check has been added to the `API_BUSINESS_PARTNER` A2X OData service. This operation can be invoked for Business Partners, Customers, and Suppliers.

Key Benefits:
This new functionality empowers external applications to identify potential duplicates before a new master data record is created via the API. By embedding this check into the creation process, organizations can prevent the introduction of redundant data at the source. This is a crucial step toward retaining high master data quality, reducing downstream cleanup efforts, and ensuring process consistency across the enterprise.

Please refer to following Blogs:

  • Using Business Partner OData API (Saranga)
  • Deep Payload for Business Partner OData API (Mani)

Duplicate Check in Business Partner A2X

 

3. Business Partner Integration: Onward Replication in a Multisystem Landscape

For organizations managing complex system landscapes, ensuring data consistency across all systems is a major undertaking. Our new onward replication capability simplifies this process significantly.

Feature Scope:
This feature allows you to trigger the onward replication of incoming business partner data from your SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP system to all other connected systems in your landscape. Activation is managed through a simple self-service configuration user interface (SSCUI).

Key Benefits:
This innovation enables your SAP S/4HANA Cloud system to function as a true master data hub. When a business partner is created or changed, the system automatically distributes these updates to all subscribed downstream systems. This automated synchronization ensures data consistency across the entire landscape, reduces manual integration efforts, and minimizes the risk of data-related process failures.

Onward Replication of Business Partner Master Data in SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP

 

Community Blog: Onward Replication of Business Partner master data in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

4. Joule as Efficient Access to Business Partner, Customer, and Supplier Master Data

In our pursuit of a more intelligent and user-friendly ERP experience, we have integrated our conversational AI, Joule, with master data management.

Feature Scope:
Joule now allows users to efficiently access attributes of Business Partner, Customer, and Supplier master data objects using natural language queries.

Key Benefits:
This feature fundamentally streamlines the process of data retrieval for business users. Instead of navigating through multiple Fiori apps and screens, a user can simply ask Joule a question, such as "Show me the addresses for Business Partner 100345" or "What are the dunning data details for Customer XYZ?". The key benefits include:

- Enhanced Productivity: Immediate access to necessary data saves time and effort, allowing analysts to focus on decision-making rather than data retrieval.
- Reduced UI Clicks: Joule provides extremely quick information about attributes without requiring users to open dedicated applications.
- Intuitive Interaction: Natural language input makes data access more accessible to a wider range of users.
BP, Customer, Supplier Master Data in SAP Joule

Joule can provide details ranging from a business partner overview, roles, and relationships to specific customer sales area data and supplier purchasing organization details.

 


Community Blog; Exploring Business Partner Master Data with SAP Joule, the AI Co-pilot

These four features represent a significant step forward in making master data management more secure, intelligent, and efficient within SAP S/4HANA Cloud. By enhancing security, improving data quality at the point of entry, simplifying landscape-wide synchronization, and accelerating user access, we are empowering our customers to build a more robust and reliable data foundation for their enterprise.

📽️ Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series! 

From January 12-16, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find the recording and presentation for LoB/Industry/Topic as well as 21 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand–anywhere, anytime. 

🔎 Find all our assets 

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: Re: SAP Service and Asset Manager Phase model Work Orders
entry.content: SSAM does not allow the user to Create Phased based Work order types from the client(Mobile app). Phase orders should not be created by mobile users. They should follow the phase processes. Orders should not be created directly to document work. Rather Either a Minor or Emergency work request should be raised. This allows for some of the phases to be skipped. SSAM supports both Minor and Emergency work requests.

entry.title: Re: Changing Stock Status from Quality Inspection to Unre...
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA, stock in Quality Inspection can only be released to Unrestricted through Quality Management processes. That’s why your attempt with MIGO (movement type 321) failed — the system enforces that inspection stock must be changed via QM, not directly through Inventory Management.

Options to resolve

Check inspection lot creation settings
Verify in material master (QM view) whether inspection type is active. If no inspection lot was created, the stock may have been posted incorrectly into inspection stock.

If inspection lot exists
Use QA32 (or Manage Usage Decisions app in Fiori) to record the usage decision. This will automatically move stock from Quality Inspection to Unrestricted (movement type 321).

If inspection lot does not exist
You cannot use QA32. So options are:

  • Post a reversal of the original goods movement (e.g., cancel GR) and repost into unrestricted.
  • Or, if business process allows, activate inspection type and create a manual inspection lot, then make usage decision.

entry.title: Re: Solution Manager SP level update (upgrade) impact on...
entry.content:
Please read KBA 3715315 - The Impact of SolMan SP Level Updates on ChaRM Operations

In the first instance, the configuration for Change Request Management is not affected by the SP level update, provided mandatory SOLMAN_SETUP activities are completed post-update. However, issues may arise if you have modified standard transaction types before the update without copying the modifications to customer namespace.

entry.title: Re: Partial payment for oversea supplier invoice and reco...
entry.content:
Good day @Prasit_Arpornrat 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, your understanding is correct. In standard AP clearing, realised FX gain or loss is posted at full clearing of the invoice item. For partial payment, there is no standard setting in public cloud to force full realised FX posting for that partial settlement portion in the same way as final clearing.

So, for your scenario, there is no standard AP process in public cloud, realised FX is tied to clearing completion. There is no standard workaround that fully replicates “realized per partial payment” without changing process design.

See the following references for more details:
https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3687934

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/abefd353ca9f4408e10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b8c08e0197454541a11f8d46ef1ab96e/ec2aa818846a43d2852598fe62b32c72.html 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP PO to SAP CI Migration - Simplifying File-Based Scenarios with SMB Adapter in SAP CI
entry.content:
@Hello @morteninge ,

Thank your for the question.

It’s true that legacy SMB versions (especially SMB1) are insecure and should not be used. It’s important to use a modern version like SMB 3.1.1 along with encryption and signing which is already mentioned in this blog.

Furthermore, The connectivity should remain strictly within internal network boundaries, From an integration perspective, access should be routed via SAP Cloud Connector(or EIC). With SAP Cloud Connector in place, Connectivity to SMB flows internally through that secure tunnel. The SMB server remains inside the network and is not publicly exposed.

Hope this clarifies.

Thanks!

entry.title: Re: Missing Employees in Executive Review | SAP SF Compensation - Executive Review | Non-EC integrat
entry.content: This sounds like it may be an RBP issue but it is difficult to confirm without seeing the system. Make sure you have an RBP role for that user and that the target population is set up to include those employees. Also, remember Executive Review only pulls data from the template so if you have these employees who are ineligible, they will not appear on forms and thus executive review. So I would first check if the users are on forms, then check RBP. Also remember that filters in executive review may have been changed and they are designed to limit visibility to specific populations. There could be other reasons but these are the most common and a good place to start.

Though have a feeling, that this case MUST BE covered by Restriction Types because they were designed especially for restrict data access.

So, put the whole case e2e here, to follow my logic chain. If i miss something (or do incorrectly) please point on this.

1 Create Data Element

2 Create Authorization Field with Data Element

3 Create Authorization Object 

4 Create Restriction Field and assign Authorization Field

5 Create Restriction Type and Assign Authorization Object and Restriction Field

6 Create Business Catalog and assign Restriction Type

7 Create Business Role and assign Business Catalog + user

8 Maintain Restrictions. As values i put Supplier numbers here

9 Create custom CDS View Entity based on I_Supplier.

10 Create Access Control by pfcg_auth, filter data by Supllier

11 Execute data preview. Here i expect to see 2 lines with Supplier = 0055300017, 0033300019. But nothing.

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud: Frequently asked questions, learn SAP HANA Cloud and take your certification
entry.content:
Why should I use a cloud database?

The top reasons for embracing cloud databases are faster access to data, consumption-based pricing, elastic scale, and a fully managed solution to reduce maintenance.

How does a cloud database help me accelerate to the cloud?

You can extend on-premise database landscapes to the cloud with expanded capacity for computing and storage to begin your cloud journey.

How does SAP HANA Cloud relate to SAP Business Technology Platform?

SAP HANA Cloud is the database management foundation of SAP Business Technology Platform. This platform encompasses database and data management, analytics, application development, and integration, as well as intelligent technologies.

 The SAP HANA Cloud adapters are different from the open connectors in SAP Business Technology Platform, however. SAP HANA Cloud provides an SAP HANA Cloud smart data integration (SDI) adapter that integrates with the open connectors framework in SAP Business Technology Platform.

How does SAP HANA Cloud relate to SAP Business Data Cloud?

Built for high performance and reliability, SAP HANA Cloud is the trusted database foundation that powers strategic solutions like SAP Business Data Cloud, ensuring your data drives innovation and insight.  SAP HANA Cloud can consume the curated data products from Business Data Cloud.

Is SAP HANA Cloud a general-purpose database?

SAP HANA Cloud is a single database that can serve many analytical and transactional use cases, including OLTP and OLAP. It adds flexibility for multimodel features, developing applications, and more.

What is the difference between SAP HANA and SAP HANA Cloud?

The SAP HANA Cloud database offers the same power and performance that you might already be familiar with in the SAP HANA database. However, SAP HANA Cloud is built natively for the cloud, which means greater connectivity and several new features. It’s also a database as a service, offering greater flexibility.

 With SAP HANA Cloud, you don’t have to worry about managing hardware, operating systems, backups, and other types of maintenance. All of this is taken care of by SAP.

Does SAP HANA Cloud only work with SAP data?

SAP HANA Cloud can access SAP and third-party data management practices, including federation and replication methods. We use smart data access (SDA) and smart data integration (SDI) and access includes ODBC, REST, data provisioning agent (DPAgent), and JDBC.

Learn more about SAP HANA Cloud with our SAP Learning:

Introducing and Preparing SAP HANA Cloud

All Courses and Learning Journeys

Live Sessions

Get and stay certified

Reimagining certification in the age of AI

SAP Certification practical exams (Step-by-step guide)

System-based assessment FAQs

Scenario-based assessment FAQs

Good learn, journey, discovery and have a good experience using our SAP Learning 

 I see you on next post.

entry.title: Bangalore Stammtisch - Jan 29
entry.content:
I was happy to be part of the 1st 2026 SAP Event at Bangalore  and it was this SAP Stammtish at the Mindset consulting office.  I reached there by 5.00 PM and the venue was already set and people started walking in. For many if you are not aware of SAP Stammtisch, it is informal gathering , Short event where people Share, Learn and Network especially with SAP background.

we were greeted by the dyanmic team of Mindset which includes @Parvathi shankar ( ParUu and @Kamalika. The team helped to settle down with initial quick introduction.

The event started with a quick Quiz in mentimeter hosted by @Vara Prasad Thatikonda ( From Daimler). This was intresting and the fastest fingers with correct answers will be the winner. It was kind of a icebreaker but there were some hard questions also .

 Post the quiz we started our discussion on the  using VS code for ABAP , then to AI application, SAP Joule, SAP BDC, Integration suite. These were great dsicussion. There were discussion on the real use case how the customer was  using the BTP services especially the SAP Translation Hub and how they saved lot of efforts. These were great insights from @vara prasad. We also heard from @Abhishek pal on the usage of the Joule in the SAP sucess factor .

The discussion really opened up some venues and it was a food for the thoughts. The time flew fast and it was already 8 Pm and many wanted to head back to home challengin the Bangalore traffic.

we ended with our ritual, that is the group photo !! We also plan to meet at the SAP Inside track 2026 at Bangalore.

Once again thank for Mindset consulting team for hosting us and being a great supporter of the SAP community.

entry.title: Re: VSCode - Unable to deploy or preview to ABAP on-prem...
entry.content:
Hi,

If you are setting HTTP and HTTPS via your operating system environment variables, then please remove the proxy config from both the ui5 and deploy YAML configurations.

To eliminate our tooling, this step might work; as it removes any third-party software as a contributing factor in your connectivity issues.

https://github.com/SAP-samples/fiori-tools-samples/tree/main/misc/proxy#validating-proxy-settings-with-curl

You can ignore the error regarding the settings.json, this is being used for other flows to ensure the appropriate settings are being migrated.

Without access to the full log, you can enable debug mode to review the HTTP requests/responses which might help understand your issues, for example on windows;

set DEBUG=* && npm run deploy

or if using PowerShell

$env:DEBUG="*"; npm run deploy

I would also suggest speaking with your IT Admin and confirming if your ABAP target host needs to be added to NO_PROXY, as not all requests need to be routed through a proxy. Please note, ERR_UNEXPECTED_PROXY_AUTH typically means:

- The client did not expect a proxy authentication challenge, or
- The authentication flow is misconfigured, unsupported, or incomplete.

John

entry.title: Timeout in SolMan/ChaRM fails to change a ticket status although import into next system succeeds
entry.content:
I've got a SolMan/ChaRM ticket referring to a big modifiable workbench transport request (with released tasks), I'm passing its status to the quality system, which creates a Transport Of Copies (ToC) which contains the object list of the original transport request, and imports it into the quality system.

While the import runs, the ChaRM Web user interface is frozen and shows in progress (spinning wheel).

After 10 minutes, there's a timeout in the ChaRM Web user interface (a timeout message is shown, probably managed by the SolMan BSP application; sorry I didn't take a screenshot).

I refresh the Web page and I see that the status remains "In Development".

I can see that the ToC is imported completely a few minutes later and has a return code 0 (success).

How to "repair" the ticket status, make it go to the next status?

Complementary question: do you know which setting to configure to increase the concerned timeout? (the one for the Web page?) It's not rdisp/max_wprun_time which is 5000 seconds.

Thanks.

Sandra

entry.title: Re: Timeout in SolMan/ChaRM fails to change a ticket status although import into next system succeed
entry.content:
Answer only to the main question. I don't know for the timeout setting.

To repair the ticket status, do this:

  1. Release and import manually the workbench transport request into the quality system
  2. Open the ChaRM ticket and change to the next status (in my case, I change two statuses: one to create and import the ToC, one to release and import the workbench transport request).
    ChaRM sees that the transport request has been released and imported successfully, so it changes the ticket status immediately without complaining.

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud 2602 / MDG - How to Mass Import Data...
entry.content:
Dear @LukasKuhn,

Question 1.

Yes these capabilities exist in Public Cloud as embedded Master Data Governance functions delivered via best-practice scope items notably:

  • 1N3 Master Data Consolidation for Business Partner
  • 1RK Mass Load and Mass Maintenance for Business Partner (In Deprecation)
  • 7MI Mass Load and Mass Maintenance for Business Partners

More details below:

https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/s4hana-cloud/master-data

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/ee9ee0ca4c3942068ea584d2f929b5b1/2d8beb7d7341406ebb9a1f33129c4d54.html?version=2508.00

Practical Public Cloud prerequisite that often matters in real systems:

  • You may need the feature toggle MDC_BP_CLOUD_READY enabled SAP explicitly references it for missing templates / setup of the new mass apps

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003660827

 Question 2.

Public Cloud best practice (SAP-supported example) export only the needed table(s) and use that export as your upload template. SAP Public Cloud Note example for inserting an Industry Sector does not export a full BP, it exports only table BUT0IS via Select Scope-> Select Tables then you maintain the required technical columns like _ACTION_CODE, SOURCE_ID, etc. and upload the file

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003506176

So the rule of thumb in Public Cloud mass processing:

  • Export the smallest set of tables that contains the data you want to add/change using Select Tables
  • Do not build a file from scratch always start from the system export because it includes the required technical columns and key fields the upload expects
  • If upload errors say table X must be included or field Y is missing that means the app requires additional dependent tables/mandatory fields for that object in that process ->re-export including the missing tables and rework using the new template

Question 3.

What SAP explicitly documents for Public Cloud BP mass apps is:

  • I = Insert
  • For updating existing entries, _ACTION_CODE is not needed leave it empty

SAP does not provide a universal public list like I/C/D/etc. for all tables, so for Public Cloud the safe statement is:

  • Guaranteed/documented I for inserts and blank for updates

Question 4.

No Public Matrix is provided that lists allowed action codes per table

In Public Cloud you generally learn this from:

  • the app validation message and/or
  • a Public Cloud Note describing the specific table/field restriction or alternative table to use

For example SAP states the Industry Sector field BUT000-IND_SECTOR is obsolete and the supported way is inserting via table BUT0IS with _ACTION_CODE = I. That’s exactly the kind of I not allowed / wrong place issue

Also, if you hit issues like template/process missing in the new mass apps, SAP points to enabling MDC_BP_CLOUD_READY

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003660827

IMPORTANT: SAP has also the separate product call SAP Master Data Governance, Cloud Edition

More details below:

https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/master-data-governance/faq

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: High Availability with Pacemaker
entry.content:
Introduction

Pacemaker’s role is to automate safe failover. It continuously monitors node health, HANA process health, and the replication state between sites. If the primary becomes unavailable, the cluster can promote the standby to primary and move the client access endpoint (typically a virtual IP or load balancer address) so applications reconnect without configuration changes. To avoid the most dangerous failure mode, dual-primary or “split brain,” the design depends on reliable fencing (STONITH) and a quorum strategy that ensures the cluster only makes promotion decisions when it can guarantee that the old primary is truly stopped.

This document describes high availability for SAP HANA using Pacemaker on Linux, focusing on a two‑site, active/passive database design with system replication and automatic failover. It explains how Pacemaker, Corosync, fencing mechanisms, and HANA system replication work together to provide fast recovery, protect against split‑brain, and ensure that failover only occurs when data is safely in sync.

 

Content Overview

  • Introduction
  • Content Overview
  • Key Terms
  • SAP ECS (RISE) reference architecture
  • HANA log shipping and log replay
  • Failover Scenarios
  • Summary
  • Documentation

 

Key Terms

Fencing (STONITH)

Fencing means forcibly removing a node from service so it cannot access shared resources or continue serving clients (classic “Shoot The Other Node In The Head”). In HANA SR clusters, fencing is primarily about preventing two primaries (data corruption risk).

Common fencing methods:

  • SBD/watchdog (very common on SLES for SAP): watchdog-triggered self-fence, optionally backed by shared/diskless SBD. SUSE explicitly stresses watchdog usage for SBD reliability.
  • IPMI/iLO, cloud fence agents, etc. SUSE provides IPMI examples.

Promotion

Pacemaker models “who is primary” as a promotable resource (historically “master/slave”). Promotion is the action: secondary is promoted to primary (takeover).

Failover

Clients (ABAP, tools, interfaces) connect to a VIP (or a cloud load balancer fronting an IP). Pacemaker moves the VIP to the node that currently hosts the primary HANA, so applications do not need to change connection endpoints. SUSE’s guide shows adding the VIP as an IPaddr2 resource and notes extra settings in public cloud.

Quorum Design

Quorum is “can this cluster make decisions safely.” Two-node clusters are special:

  • Many setups use two_node=1 and a fencing-first policy: you can run with 2 nodes, but loss of communication must result in fencing to avoid split brain. SUSE shows the corosync votequorum two-node configuration.
  • Some environments add a qdevice / “majority maker” in a third location (common for strict quorum semantics), especially for stretched scenarios.

 

SAP ECS (RISE) reference architecture

DB layer

  • Site A: HANA primary
  • Site B: HANA secondary (replication target)
  • HANA SR runs in performance-optimized style in many HA designs: one primary, one hot standby secondary continuously receiving logs (log shipping + log replay).

Cluster layer (Pacemaker + Corosync)

  • Two nodes (one per site) form a Pacemaker cluster with Corosync membership and votequorum.
  • Cluster manages:
    • Start/stop of HANA
    • Promotion logic
    • Placement of VIP
    • “Do not fail over if unsafe” rules

On SLES-for-SAP, SUSE provides dedicated SAP HANA SR resource agents and a detailed end-to-end guide.
On RHEL for SAP Solutions, Red Hat provides SAP HANA SR automation guidance and the SAP HANA-specific agents via its SAP Solutions repositories.

Fencing layer

Mandatory. If Pacemaker cannot guarantee only one side is active primary, it fences.
SUSE documents SBD/watchdog requirements and alternative STONITH methods (SBD deterministic delays, IPMI examples).
Cloud patterns (for context) often use provider fencing plus VIP via internal load balancers.

Quorum strategy

  • Often two-node votequorum mode plus fencing-first behavior. SUSE shows two_node=1 configuration.
  • Some RISE landscapes use an additional quorum mechanism (3rd site/qdevice) depending on strictness and infrastructure.

VIP / client access

VIP is controlled by Pacemaker, tied by constraints to the promoted primary. SUSE shows VIP resource configuration and constraints.

Application layer (ABAP app servers in both sites)

  • App servers in both sites are active and typically use:
    • DB connect via VIP (or LB address)
    • Logon groups and message server for user distribution
  • After a DB failover, app servers reconnect to the same endpoint, but reach the new primary.

Why SAP ECS uses this setup

  1. Fast RTO: automate takeover and VIP move.
  2. Data integrity: “fail over only if safe” and fence to prevent dual primary.
  3. Operational consistency: standardized patterns across customers, OS-vendor-supported tooling (SUSE best practices, Red Hat SAP Solutions guidance).

RHEL vs SLES differences

Common core: Pacemaker + Corosync + STONITH + VIP + HANA SR agents exist on both.

Key differences you will notice in real projects:

  • SLES for SAP
    • Strong “SAPHanaSR” ecosystem and the newer SAPHanaSR-angi, recommended for new deployments on SLES-for-SAP 15 SP4+ and later.
    • SBD/watchdog is very commonly used and heavily documented.
    • YaST tooling exists for cluster setup in SLES-for-SAP (convenience, not a requirement).

  • RHEL for SAP Solutions
    • SAP HANA SR automation is delivered via Red Hat’s SAP Solutions content and packages, and Red Hat documents extra considerations (for example systemd-based SAP startup interactions).

 

HANA log shipping and log replay

 

How HANA log shipping and log replay work

In a two-site HANA System Replication setup (performance-optimized), replication is based on redo logs, not full data pages.

Step-by-step process:

  1. Transaction commit on primary
    • A user transaction changes data in memory.
    • The change is written to the HANA redo log buffer.
    • On commit, the redo log is flushed to the primary’s log volume.

  2. Log shipping
    • The primary continuously streams redo log entries to the secondary over the SR channel.
    • This happens at kernel level, not via SQL or file copy.
    • Shipping can be synchronous or asynchronous depending on configuration.

  3. Log replay on secondary
    • The secondary receives redo entries.
    • It writes them to its own log volume.
    • The redo entries are then replayed into the secondary’s memory structures.
    • As long as replay keeps up, the secondary is transactionally consistent with the primary.

The important distinction:

  • Log shipped means the redo arrived.
  • Log replayed means the redo has been applied and the in-memory database state matches the primary.

In performance-optimized async mode, there can be a small delay between primary commit and replay on the secondary. That delay defines your RPO exposure.

 

How Pacemaker “knows” everything has been replicated

Pacemaker does not inspect database blocks or compare data pages. It relies entirely on HANA System Replication state interfaces exposed by HANA and interpreted by the HANA SR resource agents (for example SAPHanaSR / SAPHanaSR-angi on SLES or the Red Hat equivalents).

The logic works like this:

  1. Resource agent queries HANA SR status
    • The agent regularly calls HANA internal interfaces.
    • It checks:
      • Role (primary or secondary)
      • Replication mode
      • Connection status
      • Replay status
      • Synchronization state

  1. HANA classifies replication safety
    HANA internally classifies the replication state into categories such as:
    • Active / connected
    • Synchronized / in sync
    • Catch-up
    • Error / disconnected

  2. Agent maps SR state to cluster attributes
    The resource agent translates the SR state into cluster attributes like:
    • “can_promote”
    • “sync_state”
    • “sr_connected”

  3. Promotion rules use those attributes
    Pacemaker’s promotion decision is constrained by:
    • SR state must be safe
    • The node must have quorum
    • Fencing must be guaranteed

If the secondary is not in a safe synchronization state, the resource agent reports that promotion is unsafe. Pacemaker then blocks automatic takeover. This implements the safety rule you described in your document: no automatic failover if out of sync.

 

Important architectural detail

Pacemaker does not calculate LSN differences or compare timestamps itself. It trusts the HANA kernel’s SR state evaluation. That is why:

  • If monitoring paths break,
  • If HostAgent or SR interfaces are unreachable,
  • Or if quorum/fencing is degraded,

the cluster may refuse promotion even if replication looks fine from a DBA perspective. This separation of responsibilities is intentional:

  • HANA guarantees data correctness.
  • Pacemaker guarantees single-primary enforcement and controlled promotion.
  • The resource agent bridges both worlds.

 

What “safe” actually means in practice

For automatic promotion to be allowed, typically all of the following must be true:

  • Secondary is connected to primary
  • Log replay is running
  • No backlog beyond allowed thresholds
  • No replication errors
  • Cluster quorum is intact
  • Fencing is operational

If any of those checks fail, Pacemaker will refuse automatic promotion even if the primary crashes.

 

Failover Scenarios

Scenario-by-scenario – Overview

 

#

Failure scenario

Typical detection

Pacemaker action

Auto failover possible?

Main risk / note

1

HW failure primary, cannot reboot

Node disappears from Corosync

Fence (if needed), promote secondary, move VIP

Yes if secondary is “safe”

If SR not safe, promotion blocked

2

HW failure primary, reboot possible

Node loss then returns

Usually treat as failure: fence/ban, failover, keep old as secondary after repair

Yes if safe

Avoid flapping causing dual primary

3

OS crash primary, reboot fails

Node loss

Same as #1

Yes if safe

Same as #1

4

OS crash primary, reboot possible

Node loss then returns

Same as #2

Yes if safe

Must prevent old primary auto-start outside cluster

5

Storage failure primary (disk unreadable)

HANA health/resource monitor fails, FS agent may detect

Stop/fence primary if unsafe, promote secondary if safe

Sometimes

If data corruption suspected, avoid takeover

6

Network failure: primary site unreachable (from HA site)

Link loss, Corosync partition

Fence side that lost quorum per design, then promote if safe

Sometimes

Split brain risk drives fencing decision

7

Network failure: HA site unreachable (from primary)

Same as #6

Usually keep primary running, fence unreachable node if needed

No failover needed

Ensure secondary does not promote

8

OS shutdown triggered on primary

Clean stop detected (systemd/monitor)

Controlled stop, promote secondary if policy allows

Sometimes

Usually treated like planned switchover

9

HANA crash primary (crash dump)

HANA monitor fails

Restart locally if allowed; if repeated or blocked then failover

Sometimes

Depends on restart policy and SR state

10

HANA restart triggered (SIGTERM/SIGKILL)

Instance stops

Restart resource; if not stable then failover

Sometimes

Avoid promoting if SR not safe

11

HANA on HA site not responding

Secondary monitor fails

Keep primary running, attempt recovery of secondary

No

Availability ok, redundancy degraded

12

SR out of sync (log replay behind)

SR status via agents/hooks

Block takeover, alert, optionally “freeze”

No (by design)

Your “no auto failover if out of sync” rule

13

Split brain at HANA level (both primary)

SR state inconsistent + cluster policy

Immediate fencing of one side, then re-establish SR

No (automatic promotion is unsafe)

Data divergence risk

14

Pacemaker/fencing/quorum failure while HANA healthy

Cluster health checks fail

If cluster cannot guarantee safety, it may stop resources or refuse actions

No

“Cluster blind” scenario; protect data

15

Time sync failure

NTP/chrony drift alarms (often external)

Usually no immediate takeover; may stop cluster actions if policy

No (generally)

TLS/Kerberos, HANA, and cluster timing can break

16

SAP HostAgent failure

HostAgent monitor fails

Usually alert only; cluster may still run HANA

Yes/No depends

HostAgent impacts ops tooling, not always DB

 

Data Loss Risk Overview

Legend:

  • 🟢  No data loss risk
  • 🟡  Small RPO possible (async lag dependent)
  • 🔴  High / structural data loss risk
  • 🔴🔴  Divergence risk (worse than partial loss)

#

Failure Scenario

Auto Failover Typical

Data Loss Risk
(async)

Data Loss Risk
(sync)

Explanation (RPO View)

1

HW failure primary, no reboot

Yes (if safe)

🟡

🟢

Small RPO possible in async mode (redo not yet shipped/replayed)

2

HW failure primary, reboot possible

Yes (if safe)

🟡

🟢

Same as #1

3

OS crash primary, reboot fails

Yes (if safe)

🟡

🟢

Same mechanics as hardware crash

4

OS crash primary, reboot possible

Yes (if safe)

🟡

🟢

Same as #3

5

Storage failure primary

Sometimes

🔴

🔴

Possible partial loss + potential corruption risk

6

Network partition – primary unreachable

Sometimes

🔴

🔴

High risk if fencing fails or takeover forced

7

Network partition – HA unreachable

No failover

🟢

🟢

Primary continues; no takeover

8

OS shutdown primary

Sometimes

🟢 / 🟡

🟢 / 🟡

Safe if planned; small RPO if unplanned

9

HANA crash primary

Sometimes

🟡

🟢

Same as crash scenarios

10

HANA restart triggered

Sometimes

🟡

🟢

Small RPO possible if takeover before replay

11

Secondary not responding

No

🟢

🟢

No promotion; redundancy degraded only

12

SR out of sync

No (blocked)

🔴

🔴

Forced takeover causes guaranteed data loss

13

Split brain

No

🔴🔴

🔴🔴

Data divergence; one side must be discarded

14

Cluster / fencing / quorum failure

No

🟢

🟢

Cluster blocks unsafe actions

15

Time sync failure

No

🟢

🟢

No direct replication impact

16

SAP Host Agent failure

Depends

🟢

🟢

Operational impact only

If running:

  • sync or syncmem mode
    → Scenarios 1–4, 9, 10 typically move from 🟡 to 🟢 (RPO = 0)
  • async (performance-optimized)
    → Small crash-window exposure always exists
  • forced takeover while out of sync
    → Explicitly accepted data loss (scenario 12) 🔴
  • fencing failure in partition
    → Divergence possible (scenario 6 → potentially 13) 🔴

 

1 Hardware failure primary site (instant crash, reboot not possible)

Detect

  • Corosync membership loss (node gone)
  • Resource monitors fail

Sync evaluation

  • Secondary SR state checked. If it reports a safe replication state (typical “in sync” condition), takeover can proceed.

Pacemaker action

  • Fence the dead node if required by policy (sometimes “can’t fence” still counts as failed fencing and blocks, depends on setup)
  • Promote secondary
  • Move VIP to new primary

Auto failover

  • Yes, if secondary is safe.

Risk of Data Loss

  • Possible in performance-optimized (async) mode if redo logs were committed on the primary but not yet shipped/replayed.
  • No loss expected in fully synchronized (sync/syncmem) mode at crash time.
  • Exposure limited to the replication lag window (small RPO)

 

2 Hardware failure primary (crash, reboot possible)

Detect

  • Same as #1, plus the node may come back later.

Pacemaker action

  • Usually failover same as #1.
  • When the old primary returns, it must not come up as primary outside cluster control.
  • After repair, the old primary is reintroduced as secondary and SR is re-registered.

Auto failover

  • Yes, if safe.

Risk of Data Loss

  • Same technical exposure as #1.
  • Reboot possibility does not change replication risk.
  • Small RPO possible in async mode

 

3 OS crash primary (reboot fails)

Detect

  • Node loss.

Sync evaluation

  • Secondary SR state checked.

Pacemaker action

  • Same as #1.

Auto failover

  • Yes, if safe.

Risk of Data Loss

  • Same mechanics as hardware crash.
  • Possible small RPO in async mode.
  • No loss if fully synchronized at crash time.

 

4 OS crash primary (reboot possible)

Detect

  • Node loss then returns.

Pacemaker action

  • Same as #2.
  • Must prevent old primary auto-start outside cluster control.

Auto failover

  • Yes, if safe.

Risk of Data Loss

  • Same as #3.
  • Limited to replication lag window if async mode is used.

 

5 Storage failure primary site (one or more disks not readable)

Detect

  • HANA monitor reports failure (I/O errors, services stop)
  • Optional filesystem agents detect outages

Sync evaluation

  • Secondary might still be in sync, but corruption risk must be considered.

Pacemaker action

  • Stop or fence primary if unsafe
  • Promote secondary only if SR state is safe
  • If corruption suspected or SR unsafe, block promotion

Auto failover

  • Sometimes.

Risk of Data Loss

  • Elevated risk.
  • Possible partial data loss if redo not shipped.
  • Additional risk of logical corruption prior to crash.
  • In worst cases, restore from backup may be required.

 

6 Network failure, Primary Site not reachable (from HA site)

This is the classic partition case. In a standard two-node cluster with two_node=1, neither side technically "loses quorum" in the traditional sense; rather, the tie-breaking is handled by fencing. The side that survives the fencing race is the one that promotes.

Detect

  • Corosync partition.

Sync evaluation

  • HA side must not assume takeover without fencing certainty.

Pacemaker action

  • Fence the other side before promotion.
  • Fencing-first behavior critical.

Auto failover

  • Sometimes, only if fencing succeeds and SR state allows.

Risk of Data Loss

  • High risk if fencing fails or is bypassed.
  • Potential partial loss if takeover occurs without last logs.
  • Severe risk of data divergence if split brain develops.

 

7 Network failure, HA Site not reachable (from primary)

Detect

  • Same partition, seen from primary side.

Pacemaker action

  • Primary keeps running.
  • Prevent secondary from promoting.

Auto failover

  • Not applicable.

Risk of Data Loss

  • No immediate data loss risk.
  • Redundancy degraded only.

 

8 OS shutdown triggered on primary site

Typically, a clean OS shutdown without a pre-orchestrated resource move or standby command might be seen by the cluster as a failure rather than a "clean" move unless specifically configured.

Detect

  • Clean stop observed by monitors.

Sync evaluation

  • Normally treated as controlled switchover.

Pacemaker action

  • Move VIP and promote secondary before shutdown if planned.

Auto failover

  • Sometimes.

Risk of Data Loss

  • No loss if executed as planned switchover with verified sync.
  • Possible small RPO if treated as failure while not fully synchronized.

 

9 HANA crash on primary (crash dump)

 

Detect

  • SAPHana* monitor detects instance down.

Sync evaluation

  • Secondary SR status checked.

Pacemaker action

  • Attempt local restart first.
  • If restart fails, takeover if safe.

Auto failover

  • Sometimes.

Risk of Data Loss

  • Same exposure as crash scenarios.
  • Small RPO possible in async mode.
  • No loss if replay fully caught up.

 

10 HANA restart triggered on primary (SIGTERM, SIGKILL)

Operationally similar to #9, except it might be “cleaner” (SIGTERM) or abrupt (SIGKILL). Pacemaker will try restart; if not stable, it may fail over if safe.

Detect

  • Instance stops.

Pacemaker action

  • Restart resource.
  • If unstable, failover if safe.

Auto failover

  • Sometimes.

Risk of Data Loss

  • Possible if takeover occurs before replay completion.
  • Clean SIGTERM with synchronized SR typically safe.

 

 

11 HANA on HA site not responding

Detect

  • Secondary monitor fails.

Sync evaluation

  • No takeover because primary is up.

Pacemaker action

  • Keep primary running.
  • Attempt recovery of secondary.

Auto failover

  • No.

Risk of Data Loss

  • No data loss.
  • Availability intact.
  • Redundancy temporarily lost.

 

12 Primary/HA out of sync (log replay not working or falling behind)

Detect

  • SR status indicates not healthy/synchronized.

Pacemaker action

  • Block automatic takeover.
  • Alert operators.

Auto failover

  • No, by design.

Risk of Data Loss

  • Guaranteed data loss if manual forced takeover is executed.
  • Data loss equals replication lag at time of takeover.

 

13 Split brain at HANA level (both think they are primary)

Detect

  • Inconsistent SR state.
  • Impossible role combination.

Pacemaker action

  • Immediate fencing of one side.
  • Manual recovery and re-establishment of SR.

Auto failover

  • No.

Risk of Data Loss

  • Very high.
  • Data divergence possible.
  • One side’s committed transactions must be discarded.

 

14 Pacemaker/Fencing device/Quorum failure while HANA is healthy

Detect

  • Cluster services degraded.
  • STONITH errors.
  • quorum subsystem issues

Pacemaker action

  • Refuse dangerous actions.
  • Keep current primary running if possible.

Auto failover

  • No (in most sane policies).

Risk of Data Loss

  • No immediate risk.
  • Risk only arises if administrators force unsafe manual takeover.

 

15 Time synchronization failure

Detect

  • Chrony/NTP alarms.

Pacemaker action

  • Usually no immediate takeover.
  • May block cluster actions if severe.

Auto failover

  • Generally no.

Risk of Data Loss

  • No direct replication loss.
  • Indirect operational instability possible.

 

16 SAP HostAgent failure

Detect

  • Host Agent monitor fails.

Pacemaker action

  • Usually alert only.
  • HANA continues running.

Auto failover

  • Depends on design.

Risk of Data Loss

  • No direct data loss.
  • Impacts operational tooling rather than replication.

 

Summary

Failover sequence (happy path, safe SR)

  1. Primary fails
  2. Cluster detects node/resource failure
  3. Cluster fences old primary (or confirms dead)
  4.  Cluster promotes secondary to primary
  5. VIP moves to new primary
  6. ABAP app servers reconnect to same endpoint (VIP)

Where automatic failover is possible vs not

Automatic failover typically possible (if SR is safe and fencing works)

  • 1, 2, 3, 4, 16
  • 6 (only if fencing succeeds and policy allows)
  • 8, 9, 10 (depends on restart policy and SR safety)

Automatic failover typically NOT possible (blocked by safety)

  • 12 out of sync (your explicit rule)
  • 13 split brain (must fence and manually recover)
  • 14 cluster safety infrastructure broken (quorum/fencing)
  • Often 5 if corruption risk exists

Usually no failover needed (service continues)

  • 7 standby unreachable (primary still serves)
  • 11 standby HANA not responding (redundancy degraded)

Manual failover required: when and steps

When manual failover is required (common cases)

  • 12 out-of-sync SR but primary is dead or unstable and business needs recovery
  • 5 storage failure where integrity is uncertain
  • 13 split brain recovery
  • 14 fencing/quorum broken but you must move service

Manual failover steps (safe generic runbook)

  1. Stop business access to DB endpoint (freeze app tier if possible).
  2. Confirm primary is truly down or isolated.
  3. Fence old primary (or otherwise guarantee it cannot run HANA primary anymore).
  4. On the secondary, validate HANA SR status and decide:
    • If acceptable, execute HANA takeover (accepting possible data loss if not in sync).
  5. In Pacemaker, ensure the promotable resource is promoted on the intended node and VIP follows.
  6. Re-register the old site as secondary after it is repaired, then resync.

(Exact commands differ by deployment tooling, but the safety sequence above is the key.)

 

Scenarios where neither auto nor manual failover is possible

These are rare, but real:

  • Both sites down, or secondary storage also broken
  • Replication is broken and the last consistent copy is unknown
  • Severe split brain with uncertain data consistency and no trustworthy primary

In these cases, failover is not the solution.

When restore from backup is required

Most commonly:

  • Primary storage failure plus suspected corruption and secondary is not a trustworthy consistent target (5 + unsafe SR)
  • Split brain with data divergence where you cannot guarantee correctness (13)
  • Prolonged out-of-sync where logs are missing and secondary cannot be promoted without unacceptable loss (12 in worst-case)

When Pacemaker cannot reliably detect “in sync”

Pacemaker can only act on what the SR interfaces and monitoring provide. It can lose certainty when:

  • Monitoring path is broken (cluster can’t query SR state)
  • Time sync problems cause false timeouts (15)
  • HostAgent or SAP control interfaces used by the agent are unavailable (16, depending on setup)
  • Quorum/STONITH is degraded (14), so even if SR looks good, the cluster cannot guarantee single-primary enforcement

 What then?

  • Safe designs default to blocking promotion until:
    • fencing certainty is restored, and
    • SR health can be validated again

 

Documentation

Further Links and documentation are listed below:

  • An overview of supported High Availability Solutions by SLES for SAP applications

  • SAP HANA System Replication Scale-Up - Performance Optimized Scenario
  • What is SAPHanaSR-angi?
  • SAP HANA High Availability on Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) using Pacemaker
  • High availability for SAP HANA scale-out system with HSR on SUSE Linux Enterprise Server
  • Deploying Highly Available SAP NetWeaver-based Servers Using Red Hat Enterprise Linux HA add-on with Pacemaker

SAP Notes 

  • 3288971 - FAQ: SUSE HAE/RedHat HAA Pacemaker Cluster Resource Manager in SAP HANA System Replication Environments

SAP Internal

  • https://wiki.one.int.sap/wiki/spaces/HECOPS/pages/3363531559/Pacemaker+in+ECS

entry.title: New POD 2.0 Framework: Visual WYSIWYG Designer
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series on SAP Digital Manufacturing new POD 2.0 framework, the new generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing worker UIs.

  1. New POD 2.0 Framework: The New Generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing Worker UIs
  2. New POD 2.0 Framework: Template-Based Design Instead of POD Types
  3. New POD 2.0 Framework: Visual WYSIWYG Designer

The next post on the series will be published as soon as this one reaches 20 Likes, so please leave a Like! 👍

🗓️ Timeline

POD 2.0 is currently in BETA with selected customers, General Availability (GA) is planned for 2605 release. BETA content is subject to change!

📢 Visual WYSIWYG Designer

WYSIWYG stands for “What You See Is What You Get.”

It means that what you design or edit on the screen looks the same as the final result, with no surprises.

In simple terms

  • You see the layout, colors, buttons, and text exactly as users will see them
  • No need to imagine how it will render later
  • No coding or technical translation required

Example in practice

  • Drag a button → it appears there in the final UI
  • Resize a panel → workers see it resized the same way
  • Change labels or colors → instantly reflected in the real screen

Why it matters (especially for POD 2.0)

  • Faster UI creation
  • Fewer errors and rework
  • Perfect for citizen developers
  • Direct alignment with real shop-floor needs

In short: design visually, deliver instantly, and trust the outcome.

Once you've created a POD, see Template-Based Design Instead of POD Types, the next step is to configure it using the editor in the Manage PODs 2.0 app.

The WYSIWYG editor is organized around the left panel, which contains three tabs:

Pages

The Pages tab is where you can configure the pages and dialogs of your POD.

Here, you can add and delete pages or dialogs, and view a list of each. Upon choosing a page or dialog from the Pages tab, its canvas is displayed in the middle of the editor, and its properties are visible in the Properties panel.

Structure

The Structure tab lists all the plugins that are on the page or dialog you've selected.

This tab also displays the hierarchical parent child relationships between the plugins, which you can rearrange by dragging and dropping the plugins. You can search for plugins in this tab based on their ID or Plugin Type.

Plugins

In the Plugins tab, you can drag and drop plugins onto the canvas of the current page or dialog in the center of the editor.

Here, plugins are sorted by function, and you can search for specific plugins by name using the search bar. You can also right click an available plugin to see placement options relative to any plugin you have selected on the canvas.

On the right-hand side, we have another panel called Properties.

Every plugin has a Properties panel. In the Properties panel, you can review and edit the properties of the plugin you've selected in the canvas or the Structure tab. The Properties panel can contain a number of the following sections, depending on the plugin:

  • ID and Plugin Type
  • Events
  • Main
  • Accessibility
  • Appearance
  • Behavior
  • Data
  • Dimension
  • Other

Each tab provides a different way to manage and configure your POD, which you can use together with drag and drop to create and configure the desired POD look and feel.

 

In the next post of the series, I will cover hands-on how to work with POD 2.0 flexible layout capabilities.

 

The next post on the series will be published as soon as this one reaches 20 Likes, so please leave a Like! 👍

Stay tuned for more! Thanks,
Manoel Costa

entry.title: Re: Define values for Incoterm Location 1 and Incoterm Lo...
entry.content:
Complete the Vendor parameters in the /O BP transaction code. Select the BP #, Click the "Display in BP Role" field, Select the option "FLVN01 Vendor" to activate the Purchasing Menu. Click the Purchasing button at the top, and Choose a Purchase Organization for this vendor. Then verify the conditions as Currency, Payment terms, Incoterms Version and Incoterms will be populated. Click the "Partner Functions" Tab and ADD the "VN" for assigning Vendor Partner Function, and "PI" for "Invoicing Party" partner function.
Save the BP and start a new PO from Entering /N ME21N or Tile "Create Purchase Order".
This will ensure your supplier is complete for this task and eliminate the error Message no V2459.

entry.title: Re: Explanation of warehouse process tasks
entry.content:
Dear @jan_9979,

Point 1. When does the system determine the WPT?

For warehouse request documents inbound/outbound deliveries, posting change, etc. the system determines a WPT per item during creation/activation of the warehouse request.
If you use process-oriented storage control (POSC) / work center steps the WPT can also come from or be overridden by the process step when the next warehouse task is created

More details below version strictly for Public Cloud WM but also for EWM because it is very well described and mostly overlaps with Public Cloud:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/basic-customizing-in-sap-s-4hana-ewm/applying-warehouse-process-types

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-warehouse-management/defining-the-impact-of-warehouse-management-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition_e5067b97-e366-40d6-a949-d13edfdfe71c

Point 2. Which fields can determine the WPT?

In the CBC Determine Warehouse Process Type (104392) the key fields you can use and which SAP content also uses are:

  • Warehouse number (always)
  • Document type
  • Item type
  • Delivery priority
  • Control indicator for process type determination (PTDI) comes from the warehouse product master
  • Process indicator to further differentiate processes
  • Warehouse proces type

Also you can use help icon

to see more details like below per column:

And next option is open documentation

Point 3. The access logic

SAP standard access logic is a most-specific-to-least-specific fallback. Assumptions:

  • Item type is more specific than document type
  • Delivery priority is more specific than the product control indicator (PTDI)
  • Warehouse number is always considered

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/basic-customizing-in-sap-s-4hana-ewm/applying-warehouse-process-types

Fallback sequence simplified exactly as SAP documents it:

  1. Doc + Item + Priority + Indicator
  2. Doc + Item + Priority
  3. Doc + Item + Indicator
  4. Doc + Item
  5. Doc + Priority + Indicator
  6. Doc + Priority
  7. Doc + Indicator
  8. Doc only
  9. Item + Priority + Indicator
  10. Item + Priority
  11. Item + Indicator
  12. Item only

So if you didn’t create your own entries the system still selects a WPT from pre-delivered SAP content using exactly this fallback

Point 4. How to view the standard SAP content in Public Cloud

You can see the delivered determination records (read-only) which is usually the missing piece:

  • Open Implementation Activities app in Public Cloud tenant or in CBC Determine Warehouse Process Type (104392)
  • In the filter allow showing read-only
  • Navigate to like on the screen:

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3653923

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: The Hidden Threat to Your Clean Core: Variant Governance in S/4HANA

My expertise lies in S/4HANA Transformations, Clean Core strategies, and BTP-enabled AI. I would value your feedback on the topics the community is currently most interested in exploring. Specifically, I am considering discussing:

I would appreciate any suggestions on which of these (or other areas) would provide the most value to our members.

entry.title: Re: Workforce replication from SuccessFactors to S4/Hana Public Cloud using Master Data Integration
entry.content:
Hey @Kirthan_Kumar ,

Thanks for this comprehensive guide, it was of great value to us.

One thing we stumbled upon and might need adjustment: we were not able to find (nor activate) scope item 4AF. We reached out to SAP Support and they mentioned the following:

instead of scope item 4AF which was deprecated, to integrate SuccessFactors ECP and SAP S/4HANA Cloud you can use the standard way via MDI.

 We now have a successful integration up-and-running without scope item 4AF activate.

Secondly, you mention the /WFS workspace, I think this is supposed to be /WFD. Using this workspace in combination with the 'Message Monitoring' app was of much use to us to debug records with missing or incorrect master data (e.g. incorrect company codes, missing email addresses, etc.).

Kind regards,

Casper

entry.title: SAP CAL portal freezes after login
entry.content:
Hello

I am trying to access the SAP Cloud Appliance Library (CAL).

The authentication works successfully — I log in using my SAP Universal ID — however, after the login redirect, the page remains stuck on a loading screen and does not proceed to the dashboard.

The screen only shows a message: "SAP Cloud Appliance Library is loading, please wait..." and does not allow interaction.

What I already tried:

  • Different browsers (Chrome and Edge)

  • Incognito/private window

  • Cleared cookies and cache

  • Different network

  • Disabled extensions/ad-blockers

The issue persists in all cases.

Could you please check if there is a problem with my account or tenant access?

 

I need use to create a new instance sap

entry.title: Has SAP Quietly Removed Secure Login Service from the Discovery Center?
entry.content:
The SAP Secure Login Service has disappeared from the SAP Discovery Center without any visible announcement or explanation. This was the original link: https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/serviceCatalog/sap-secure-login-service?service_plan=default&region=all&commercialModel=subscription

It used to provide essential information such as available regions, hyperscaler support, and service plan details. For architects and customers, this was a key reference point.

Right now, the only remaining official resource I could find is this product page: https://www.sap.com/germany/products/financial-management/secure-login-service-for-gui.html

Is this a temporary catalog change, a restructuring under SAP Cloud Identity Services, or something else?

Would appreciate any clarification from SAP or the community 

THX Cheers Colt

entry.title: Re: Automating Invoice Predictions from SAP Databricks with SAP Datasphere Task Chain
entry.content:
@BabacarT  Excellent deep dive, Babacar! This end-to-end orchestration is a textbook example of how to leverage SAP Datasphere to bridge the gap between specialized ML environments like Databricks and the Business Data Cloud.

From an architectural standpoint, using Task Chains for asynchronous API calls is the right move to ensure a Clean Core—keeping the heavy processing out of the ERP while maintaining near real-time visibility for Finance Managers.

I’m curious, have you explored using the SAP BTP AI Foundation as a secondary consumption layer for these predictions?

entry.title: Re: Explanation of warehouse process tasks
entry.content:
Below would be the Determination logic

When the system creates a warehouse task:

  • It checks if the inbound is HU‑managed.
  • It checks if auto WT creation is active.
  • If both are true → S127 is selected.
  • If HU‑managed but auto WT is off → S126.
  • If not HU‑managed → one of the non‑HU WPTs (S110, S116, S117, S012).
  • If source is production → S115.
  • S127 is essentially the “automatic HU putaway” flavor of the standard inbound putaway process.

entry.title: Re: 10 reasons to trust the SAP Business Transformation Center with your data migration
entry.content:
Thank you for the detailed insights! Your point about the iterative evolution of S/4HANA vs. the 'static template' of the past decade is a critical mindset shift for the industry.

It’s also encouraging to hear that SAP BTC is bridge-building between Basis and Functional teams; that 'guided UX' is exactly what we need to scale these transformations. These discussions are incredibly motivating—they definitely push me to dig deeper into the 'under-the-hood' capabilities of BTC to better serve my clients. Looking forward to seeing how the predefined content continues to evolve for the more complex edge cases!
